bins.name: Federal authority is limited
bins.desc: North Carolina Public Staff challenges FERC's jurisdictional authority to regulate load interconnections, arguing that retail service, generation, and rate-setting are reserved to the states by Congress. The comments warn that federal rules cannot address the core problem (generation supply) and risk infringing on state authority over retail rates, generation siting, and curtailment terms.
quotes: it is doubtful that the Commission has authority to order the reforms outlined in the ANOPR, and if it does, its authority is quite narrow.

Interconnection of load into the transmission system might, in a technical sense, be within FERC's jurisdiction; however, this is an area in which most, if not all, of the authority has been reserved to the states by Congress.

A Commission rule prescribing the methods, timing, and transmission pricing for interconnecting load to the transmission system could not, at the end of the day, provide any right for that load to be actually served as a retail customer, a matter unquestionably reserved to the states alone.

Because federal jurisdiction does not extend to facilities used for the generation of electric energy, the rules envisioned by the ANOPR cannot reach the heart of the problem they wish to address. A transmission study of a load that cannot be served is of no value.

any final rule should place no constraints, express or implied, on the authority of states to set the terms and rates for retail customers over generation facilities, or over the siting of transmission.
The ANOPR proposal to provide special accommodation to load that agrees to be curtailed is an example of a rule that quickly infringes on state jurisdiction.

Bundled retail rates that include transmission should be preserved. When the Commission issued Order No. 888, it took no action to require these rates to be unbundled, in part to avoid difficult jurisdictional issues.

bins.name: Coordinated studies risk delays
bins.desc: The filing argues that adding large load interconnections to transmission study processes will cause restudies, delays, and inaccurate studies, particularly when projects drop out or change profiles. The staff recommends deferring final rules until NERC completes its reliability guidelines for large loads.
quotes: Because federal jurisdiction does not extend to facilities used for the generation of electric energy, the rules envisioned by the ANOPR cannot reach the heart of the problem they wish to address. A transmission study of a load that cannot be served is of no value.

Even with reasonable study processes in place to minimize speculative projects entering the load queue, adding a new layer to the study process will inevitably cause restudies, delays, and inaccurate studies when projects inevitably either drop out of the queue or seek material changes to their load profiles.

the North Carolina Public Staff recommends that if the Commission promulgates a rule on large load interconnection, that it limit its applicability to loads in excess of 300 MW. This would reduce the number of load customers requiring study and limit the projects studied to those most likely to have significant impacts on the bulk electric system.

While NERC is assiduously studying reliability issues associated with serving large loads and preparing to issue guidelines, issuing final rules prior to those guidelines being finalized would be counterproductive.

bins.name: Preserve cost visibility
bins.desc: North Carolina Public Staff argues that bundled retail rates (including transmission costs) should remain bundled to preserve state regulatory transparency and oversight. Federal disaggregation of transmission pricing for large loads would undermine state consumer advocates' ability to audit utility costs.
quotes: A Commission rule prescribing the methods, timing, and transmission pricing for interconnecting load to the transmission system could not, at the end of the day, provide any right for that load to be actually served as a retail customer, a matter unquestionably reserved to the states alone.

Bundled retail rates that include transmission should be preserved. When the Commission issued Order No. 888, it took no action to require these rates to be unbundled, in part to avoid difficult jurisdictional issues.

bins.name: Curtailment terms are state matter
bins.desc: The filing opposes federal rules that provide special accommodation or incentives for loads that agree to curtailment, as such terms directly intrude on state authority to set retail service conditions.
quotes: The ANOPR proposal to provide special accommodation to load that agrees to be curtailed is an example of a rule that quickly infringes on state jurisdiction.

bins.name: 300 MW threshold proposal
bins.desc: North Carolina Public Staff recommends that any federal rule limit its applicability to loads exceeding 300 MW, which would reduce study volume, focus on systemic impacts, reduce jurisdictional concerns (as such loads are unlikely to connect to distribution), and limit collateral impacts on generation interconnection.
quotes: the North Carolina Public Staff recommends that if the Commission promulgates a rule on large load interconnection, that it limit its applicability to loads in excess of 300 MW. This would reduce the number of load customers requiring study and limit the projects studied to those most likely to have significant impacts on the bulk electric system.
